prompt: Which statement best captures the difference between a real value metric and a vanity metric for an AI system?
choices: A real value metric ties to a business outcome (time, money, risk, quality); a vanity metric looks impressive but does not
A vanity metric is always a larger number than a real value metric
A real value metric is anything reported in a dashboard; a vanity metric is anything reported in a slide
There is no difference; both measure how often the model is called

prompt: Select ALL of the following that are signals it is time to KILL (stop) an AI pilot rather than scale it.
choices: Its value metric never clears the pre-agreed threshold despite real effort
Adoption stays low even after training and support
The cost to run it at higher volume exceeds the benefit it delivers
The pilot cleared its value threshold and agents keep using it without being told to

prompt: Put the stages of taking an AI capability from idea to wide use in the correct order.
choices: Pilot, then production, then scale
Scale, then pilot, then production
Production, then scale, then pilot
Scale, then production, then pilot

prompt: A PM/Product Owner reports that an AI drafting tool is technically working and 'available to all 40 agents,' but realised value is near zero. Which response best reflects how this role should use AI value and scaling thinking?
choices: Conclude the AI failed and shut it down immediately, since availability did not create value
Treat this as an adoption gap and lead change management — training, trust, clear workflows — while measuring active and repeat users, not just availability
Report 'available to all agents' as the success metric and consider the rollout complete
Increase the model's accuracy, since accuracy is the only thing that drives value

prompt: A pilot shows modest but real value, yet the unit economics look shaky at full scale. Which scaling *trade-off* is the disciplined choice?
choices: Roll out to everyone immediately — it works, so scale is the obvious next step
Fix the unit economics first (or scale a narrow high-value slice) before a full rollout
Kill it outright; modest value is never worth pursuing
Keep it in pilot forever to avoid the decision
